items.title: LeetCode journal: Remove Duplicates from array
items.author: Vinicius Basilio
items.content:
When I started these posts, I was just having fun as a hobbyist programmer, but recently I started to CS in college, so my posts will become more technical.

For this serie, I wanna post my LeetCode solutions; everytime when I achive 100% in runtime analysis or at least 80% in memory efficiency, I will share my code here.

Posts will follow leetcode model:

Another simple two pointers trick. Let a pointer for the first item of array (call it index). Then use another pointer to search, keep waiting a different item to appear. At this time, just increase by one our index pointer and copy the value pointed by the other one.

We will build a "for loop", is easy to loop through array with this loop. We'll need to mutable vars, one to hold our "lazy" pointer, the one that memorized the next index, and another for search, this var will be updated just when the index value became different from the search pointer.

items.title: Python Performance Profiling: 7 Techniques to Optimize Your Code in 2023
items.author: Aarav Joshi
items.content:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world!

Python performance profiling is essential for identifying bottlenecks and optimizing code efficiency. I've used these techniques extensively in production environments to transform sluggish applications into responsive systems. Let me share what I've learned about each approach, with practical examples you can apply immediately.

Understanding Python Performance Profiling

Performance profiling is the systematic measurement of how your code executes - analyzing time spent in functions, memory usage patterns, and resource consumption. Python, being an interpreted language, has unique performance characteristics that benefit tremendously from profiling.

When profiling Python applications, I focus on answering key questions: Which functions consume the most time? Where are memory allocations occurring? Are there unnecessary function calls? The answers guide targeted optimization efforts rather than premature optimization.

Time-Based Profiling with cProfile

The cProfile module is Python's built-in profiler for measuring execution time. It's my first choice when investigating performance issues because it provides detailed statistics with minimal setup.

This produces a detailed report showing calls, time per call, and cumulative time. I usually sort by cumulative time to identify functions consuming the most resources.

For longer-running applications, I use the context manager approach:

When cProfile identifies problematic functions, I drill down further with line-level profiling using the line_profiler package. This reveals exactly which lines consume time within a function.

The output shows time spent on each line, making it clear where optimizations will yield the greatest benefits. I've often found surprising bottlenecks this way, like string concatenation in loops that could be replaced with join() operations.

Memory Profiling Techniques

Memory issues can be more challenging to diagnose than speed problems. I rely on memory_profiler to track memory consumption line by line:

The decorator shows memory usage for each line, revealing where large allocations occur. This has helped me identify unnecessary data duplication and opportunities for generators instead of lists.

For tracking object creation and reference patterns, Python's built-in tracemalloc module is invaluable:

This approach has helped me find memory leaks in long-running applications that would otherwise be difficult to diagnose.

Visualizing Performance with Flame Graphs

Converting profiling data into visual representations makes patterns more apparent. Flame graphs have transformed how I analyze performance issues:

For code already running, py-spy is non-intrusive and generates SVG flame graphs showing the call stack and time distribution. The wider a function appears in the graph, the more time it consumes.

Another visualization approach I use with cProfile data:

SnakeViz creates interactive visualizations of cProfile data, making it easier to explore the call hierarchy and identify performance bottlenecks.

Benchmarking Code Segments

For comparing implementation alternatives, I use the timeit module to run micro-benchmarks:

For more complex benchmarking scenarios, pytest-benchmark provides statistical analysis and historical tracking:

Development environment profiling can miss real-world issues. For production monitoring, I implement sampling profilers with minimal overhead:

This approach collects stack traces periodically with minimal performance impact, suitable for production systems.

Optimizing Database Interactions

Many Python applications interact with databases, which can be a major performance bottleneck. I profile these interactions with query logging and timing:

For ORMs like SQLAlchemy, I enable query logging to identify N+1 query problems and opportunities for bulk operations.

Optimizing Python Code Based on Profiling Results

After collecting profiling data, the real work begins. Here are patterns I frequently implement:

  1. Replace inefficient data structures:
  1. Reduce function call overhead with local variables:
  1. Use generators for processing large datasets:
  1. Implement caching for expensive computations:

I recently optimized a data processing pipeline that was taking over 40 minutes to complete. Using cProfile, I identified that JSON serialization and database queries were the primary bottlenecks.

The optimization process:

  1. First, I profiled the application:
  1. The results showed excessive database queries. I implemented batch processing:
  1. JSON processing was also slow, so I replaced the standard library with a faster alternative:
  1. Final verification with profiling showed a 10x improvement, reducing runtime to under 4 minutes.

Continuous Profiling Practices

I've found that integrating profiling into development workflows pays dividends. Techniques I use include:

  1. Adding performance tests to CI/CD pipelines:

  1. Scheduled profiling runs in staging environments to catch gradual degradations.

  2. Automated reports comparing performance metrics between releases.

By consistently applying these profiling techniques, I've been able to achieve significant performance improvements in Python applications. The key is not just collecting data but understanding what it tells you about your code's behavior and applying targeted optimizations where they matter most.

items.title: Golang - How to do Benchmarking
items.author: Ankit malik

Performance matters — especially when dealing with large-scale systems or performance-critical code. Go (Golang) provides first-class support for benchmarking via the package. This article walks you through the fundamentals of benchmarking in Go: what it is, why it's important, and how to run benchmarks to compare multiple functions.

is the process of measuring how efficiently a piece of code executes. In Go, this typically involves determining how long a function takes to run, how many resources it uses, or how well it scales under load.

Benchmarking is different from testing — it doesn’t check if your code is correct but it checks how well it performs.

✅ Why We Should Do Benchmarking

Benchmarking is useful when you want to:

  • Compare different implementations of the same logic (e.g., using vs for string concatenation).
  • Find performance bottlenecks in your application.
  • over time, especially in performance-sensitive systems.
  • Make data-driven decisions when optimizing code.

In short, benchmarking gives you into your code’s behavior under stress.

Let’s take an easy and real world example which we often overlook

Save the file as and run:

Benchmarking in Go is easy to set up and incredibly valuable. It helps you:

  • Understand the performance characteristics of your code
  • Choose the most efficient implementations
  • Catch regressions before they ship to production

By comparing two simple functions, we saw how small differences in implementation can lead to measurable performance improvements.

items.title: Why Use train() in nn.Module When forward() is Called?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
When working with PyTorch, a common question arises: "Does calling a model invoke the forward() method in nn.Module? Why is there a need to specify train()?" Understanding this concept is crucial for effectively using PyTorch for deep learning.

Understanding nn.Module in PyTorch

The class in PyTorch is a base class for all neural network modules. It provides essential functionalities for training models, including parameter management, saving/loading, and more. The method defines the forward pass of the model, where the input is processed to produce the output.

Why the forward() Method?

In PyTorch, when you instantiate a model that inherits from and call it with some input data, it implicitly calls the method. This occurs due to Python’s special method lookup. When you create a model and deal with tensors, using the model instance on an input triggers the following sequence:

  1. The call to the model instance (e.g., output = model(input_tensor)).
  2. Internally, this invokes the method of the model, which then calls . So you are correct; indeed, when you call the model, the method is used.

The Importance of train()

The and methods are critical for setting the mode of the model. They adjust the behavior of certain layers (like dropout and batch normalization) based on whether the model is in training or evaluation mode.

What Happens When train() is Invoked?

  • : When you set your model to training mode using , it enables dropout layers to randomly drop neurons during the forward pass. This helps in regularization and prevents overfitting. It also configures batch normalization to use the current batch statistics.

  • : Conversely, using switches the model into inference mode. Dropout layers are turned off, and batch normalization uses running estimates instead of batch statistics. This ensures that the results are consistent and reproducible during evaluation and inference.

Example: Using nn.Module with train() and forward()

To illustrate these concepts, here’s a basic PyTorch example showing how to implement a custom neural network model with both and modes:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

# Define a simple neural network model
class SimpleNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(SimpleNN, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(10, 5)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(5, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.fc1(x)
        x = self.relu(x)
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x

# Create an instance of the model
model = SimpleNN()

# Set the model to training mode
model.train()

# Create a random input tensor
input_tensor = torch.randn((1, 10))

# Forward pass in training mode
output_train = model(input_tensor)
print("Output in train mode:", output_train)

# Set the model to evaluation mode
model.eval()

# Forward pass in evaluation mode
output_eval = model(input_tensor)
print("Output in eval mode:", output_eval)

Key Points from the Example

  1. : The model is defined inheriting from with a custom method.
  2. Training and Evaluation Modes: The method is called to prepare the model for training, while switches it to the evaluation phase.
  3. : The outputs in both modes can be compared, highlighting changes in dropout behavior or batch normalization.

Frequently Asked Questions

1. What is the difference between train() and eval()?

The method enables training-specific features, while sets the model for inference, disabling dropout and using running statistics.

2. Do I need to call train() every time I switch modes?

Yes, it's good practice to specify the mode each time you need to switch between training and evaluation.

3. Can I modify the behavior of forward()?

Yes, you can customize the logic within the method to accommodate specific needs for your model’s architecture.

Understanding the and methods in conjunction with the method is essential for successfully navigating the training and inference phases of a PyTorch model. Always remember to configure your model appropriately before each phase to leverage the full power of PyTorch's capabilities.

By grasping these concepts, you can enhance your deep learning workflows, ensure better model performance, and potentially achieve improved results in your projects.

items.title: Creating Declarative APIs with Class Decorators in Python
items.author: Heval Hazal Kurt
items.content:
When you use libraries like SQLAlchemy, Pydantic, or FastAPI, you might notice that you can declare behavior just by writing classes with certain decorators. This style is called declarative programming. You describe what you want, and the system figures out how to do it. One of the ways this magic happens in Python is through class decorators.

In this blog post, we’ll walk through what class decorators are, how they can be used to build declarative APIs, and build a simple example from scratch to make the idea clear.

What is a Class Decorator?

Let’s start with a quick refresher. A class decorator is a function that takes a class object as input and returns a possibly modified or wrapped class.

When Python sees , it runs . This is very similar to function decorators, but the input is a class.

What’s a Declarative API?

A lets the developer express intent with very little code, often without explicitly invoking logic. For example:

Here, might register this class as a handler for a web request. The user doesn't need to know how routing works, just declare the intent.

Building a Mini Declarative System

Let’s build a simple example where we can declare configuration for services using class decorators. Imagine we want to register all services that provide some functionality and access their metadata later.

We need a place to keep track of services:

Step 2: The Class Decorator

Now, let’s make a decorator that registers any class with a name and optional config:

Step 3: Declaring Services

Now, we’ve created a way to define services. All metadata is stored automatically:

: : <class ,
    : : 3, : 5,
  : : <class ,
    : :
  • We created a for services.
  • We used a to capture metadata and register classes.
  • Developers can now declare new services just by decorating a class.

They don’t need to know anything about how the registry works, it’s declarative.

You can do much more with class decorators:

  • Automatically inject configuration into
  • Wrap or replace class methods
  • Add new class-level behavior dynamically

For example, to inject config into a class:

Real-World Examples in Frameworks

Many popular Python frameworks use this pattern:

  • uses decorators like to register route handlers declaratively.
  • uses or model classes to declare schemas and validation rules.
  • uses declarative base classes to define ORM models.

Once you understand class decorators, you start to see the pattern behind these intuitive APIs.

Class Decorators vs Metaclasses

Class decorators are great for simple behavior injection and registration. They:

  • Are easier to write and understand
  • Don't require touching inheritance chains

Metaclasses are more powerful, but:

  • Can be overkill for many cases

Use class decorators when you want to keep things readable, and reach for metaclasses when you need low-level control over class creation itself.

Adding a Utility: Service Instantiation

You can also add a helper function to instantiate registered services:

This makes the system not only declarative but also easy to use dynamically.

Writing Tests for Your Decorators

Here’s a simple unit test for the registry logic:

Or test the injected config:

Tests help ensure your decorators behave as expected when used in larger systems.

Common Pitfalls to Watch Out For

  • : If you wrap the class, the name and docstring may change. Preserve them manually if needed.
  • : If your decorator changes global state (like a registry), be sure it's thread-safe.
  • Returning the wrong object: Always return the new or original class from the decorator.
  • Forgetting : When wrapping classes, don't forget to call the original constructor.

Class decorators are a powerful tool in Python, especially when designing APIs that feel intuitive and expressive. They let you shift from writing code that says do this to code that says this is what I want.

If you’re building tools, frameworks, or even internal SDKs, consider how class decorators might help you build clean, declarative APIs that your future self and your teammates will thank you for.

items.title: How to Access Currently Open Windows in Rust on Windows?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
Building a Windows application in Rust can be both exciting and challenging, especially when it involves managing window visibility and styles. If you're trying to access all currently open windows, understanding how the visibility of a window is determined through its styles and state is crucial. In this article, we'll explore how to leverage the EnumWindows function along with a callback to filter out windows based on their visibility and styles within a Rust application.

Understanding Window Visibility and Styles

When working with the Windows API, visibility and styles are defined by specific properties of the window, accessible through functions like and . The function checks if a window is visible, returning a boolean value. However, it may not always accurately reflect whether a window is usable or actively displayed on the screen, especially if there are background or hidden windows that report they are visible.

Window styles, on the other hand, are stored as a bitmask, indicating various behaviors of the window, such as whether it's a modal dialog, has a caption, etc. These styles are represented using hexadecimal values, which can seem confusing at first but are simply another way of expressing binary flags.

In your case, you've noticed that visible windows generally have positive style values while invisible ones display negative values. This discrepancy suggests that you might encounter windows considered visible by the function but are actually not usable. This is why you implemented the check, which ensures you're only processing windows with a valid (positive) style.

Implementing the EnumWindows Callback

Let's dive into the Rust code to implement the function with a more in-depth explanation:

use windows::Win32::Foundation::{BOOL, HWND, LPARAM};
use windows::Win32::UI::WindowsAndMessaging::{EnumWindows, GetWindowLongW, GWL_STYLE, GetWindowTextW, IsWindowVisible};

unsafe extern "system" fn enum_callback(hwnd: HWND, _: LPARAM) -> BOOL {
    let window_visible = IsWindowVisible(hwnd).as_bool();
    let window_style = GetWindowLongW(hwnd, GWL_STYLE);

    // Filter based on window visibility and valid style
    if window_visible && window_style > 0 {
        let mut win_text = [0u16; 128];
        GetWindowTextW(hwnd, &mut win_text);
        let window_title = String::from_utf16_lossy(&win_text);
        println!("VISIBLE: {}", window_title.trim());
    }
    BOOL::from(true) // Continue enumeration
}

pub fn main() {
    unsafe {
        EnumWindows(Some(enum_callback), LPARAM(0));
    }
}

  1. : The example imports necessary modules from the crate to access Windows API functions. Make sure you have this crate in your dependencies.

  2. : This API function enumerates all top-level windows on the screen and calls the specified callback function for each window. The function is used to evaluate each window.

  3. Visibility and Style Checks: Within , the visibility of each window is checked using and the window's style is retrieved with . If both conditions are satisfied (the window is visible and has a valid positive style), it retrieves the window's title and prints it.

  4. : The use of is necessary because interacting with raw pointers and external functions poses risks that Rust's safety guarantees don't cover.

The style constants in the Windows API are captured in hexadecimal format because this representation succinctly expresses multiple bits at once, allowing for easier manipulation. Each bit corresponds to a specific behavior or property of the window. For instance, the WS_VISIBLE bit is the first bit in the style, meaning if it’s set, the window is intended to be visible. The use of hexadecimal is a common practice in low-level programming when bitwise operations are heavily utilized.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do some invisible windows still register as visible?

Typically, windows that are minimized or in the background may still be marked as visible. Thus, they may pass the check despite not being usable to the user.

Is there a way to further filter invisible windows?

Yes, you can implement additional checks such as checking if the window is minimized using . This further refines your search for truly visible windows.

What are style constants and why are they important?

Style constants are bit masks that represent specific behaviors and attributes of windows. Understanding these is crucial for efficient window management in applications.

By effectively using the function alongside visibility and style checks, you can accurately list only those windows that meet your criteria. This approach improves the usability of your Rust application by filtering out unnecessary windows, ensuring that your users interact only with those that are contextually significant. Understanding window styles and their significance is crucial when developing applications that rely on GUI interactions. With the techniques shared in this blog post, you can confidently manage window visibility in your Rust applications.

items.title: Ned Batchelder: PyCon summer camp
items.content:
I’m headed to PyCon today, and I’m reminded about how it feels like summer camp, in mostly good ways, but also in a tricky way.

You take some time off from your “real” life, you go somewhere else, you hang out with old friends and meet some new friends. You do different things than in your real life, some are playful, some take real work. These are all good ways it’s like summer camp.

Here’s the tricky thing to watch out for: like summer camp, you can make connections to people or projects that are intense and feel like they could last forever. You make friends at summer camp, or even have semi-romantic crushes on people. You promise to stay in touch, you think it’s the “real thing.” When you get home, you write an email or two, maybe a phone call, but it fades away. The excitement of the summer is overtaken by your autumnal real life again.

PyCon can be the same way, either with people or projects. Not a romance, but the exciting feeling that you want to keep doing the project you started at PyCon, or be a member of some community you hung out with for those days. You want to keep talking about that exciting thing with that person. These are great feelings, but it’s easy to emotionally over-commit to those efforts and then have it fade away once PyCon is over.

How do you know what projects are just crushes, and which are permanent relationships? Maybe it doesn’t matter, and we should just get excited about things.

I know I started at least one effort last year that I thought would be done in a few months, but has since stalled. Now I am headed back to PyCon. Will I become attached to yet more things this time? Is that bad? Should I temper my enthusiasm, or is it fine to light a few fires and accept that some will peter out?

items.title: Top Debt Recovery Strategies for Small Business Owners
items.author: VIP Arabian Safari
items.content:
As a small business owner, cash flow is one of the most critical aspects of your business’s success. When clients delay or fail to make payments, it can put immense pressure on your operations, impacting everything from paying bills to investing in growth. Unfortunately, debt is a common issue, and recovering it can be one of the most challenging tasks a business owner faces. However, handling small business debt recovery doesn’t have to be painful or confrontational. By employing the right strategies, you can recover debts while maintaining positive client relationships and ensuring business continuity.

In this guide, we will explore the most effective debt recovery strategies small business owners can adopt. These approaches will help ensure that overdue payments are collected in a professional, systematic, and respectful manner.

  1. Establish Clear Payment Terms Upfront The best way to avoid debt-related issues in the future is to prevent them from happening in the first place. This begins with clear and transparent payment terms established before any work or transaction begins. Steps to Take: Set Clear Payment Terms in Contracts: Always include the payment due date, penalties for late payments, and payment methods in your contracts. Specify whether you expect full payment upon delivery or partial payments with agreed-upon milestones.

Discuss Terms with Clients: Before you begin, have a conversation with clients about the terms, ensuring that they are on board with the payment schedule and penalties.

Use Detailed Invoices: Provide clients with invoices that are clear and specific, showing exactly what they are being charged for, the due date, and the payment options.

By setting expectations from the start, you significantly reduce the chances of confusion or disputes down the road.

  1. Invoice Promptly and Professionally Once the work is completed or the products are delivered, sending out an invoice promptly is key to initiating the payment process. Delayed invoices can confuse clients or make them feel that the payment deadline is flexible. Tips for Professional Invoicing: Send Invoices Immediately: The moment the work is complete or the product is shipped, send the invoice. This ensures that clients are aware of the charges and due dates.

Use Online Invoicing Software: Consider using tools like QuickBooks, FreshBooks, or Zoho, which automate the invoicing process and allow you to track the status of your invoices.

Be Clear and Accurate: Make sure your invoices include all necessary information, such as client name, your business name, details of the work performed, the total amount due, payment instructions, and the due date.

A well-organized invoice not only aids the payment process but also reflects your professionalism, which can positively impact your client relationship.

  1. Send Friendly Payment Reminders Sometimes clients simply forget or overlook payments, and a friendly reminder can encourage them to settle their debts. Regular follow-ups are essential to maintaining cash flow without damaging client relationships. How to Follow Up: Send a Reminder Before the Due Date: A gentle reminder a few days before the payment due date can ensure that clients are prepared to pay on time. A simple “heads up” email or message is effective.

Follow Up Immediately After the Due Date: If a payment is missed, send a polite email thanking the client for their business and gently reminding them of the overdue invoice. Include a copy of the invoice in case it was misplaced.

Use Multiple Channels: Some clients may prefer emails, while others may respond better to phone calls. Don’t hesitate to use different communication channels (email, phone calls, text messages, etc.) to remind clients.

Timing and tone are crucial. Keep the message respectful and avoid being confrontational, as it can damage the relationship.

  1. Offer Payment Plans or Installments Some clients may struggle with making a lump sum payment due to their own cash flow issues. Offering them a payment plan can not only increase the likelihood of getting paid, but it also shows that you care about their business and are willing to be flexible. How to Structure Payment Plans: Offer Installments: Let the client pay in smaller amounts over a set period. For example, they might pay 25% of the balance every 30 days until the full amount is paid.

Negotiate Terms: Tailor payment plans based on the client’s financial situation, but ensure you don’t extend the terms too far out. Set clear expectations about the timing and interest (if any).

Get It in Writing: Always confirm payment arrangements in writing to prevent any confusion or future misunderstandings.

A payment plan can be a win-win: you recover the debt, and your client doesn’t feel overwhelmed by a large upfront payment.

  1. Implement Late Payment Fees Late payment fees are an effective way to encourage clients to settle debts quickly. When clients are aware that delays will result in added costs, they are more likely to prioritize their payments. Guidelines for Late Fees: Include Late Fee Clauses in Contracts: Ensure that clients are aware of late payment penalties before the work begins. These fees should be clearly mentioned in your service agreements or contracts.

Be Reasonable: Late fees should be reasonable—typically 1% to 5% of the total invoice amount per month. A small fee will encourage clients to pay on time without creating unnecessary tension.

Give a Grace Period: Offering a short grace period (5–10 days) before late fees are applied can show flexibility, while still incentivizing timely payments.

Late fees are a reminder that paying on time has financial benefits and that delays can be costly.

  1. Seek Mediation Before Legal Action In cases where clients consistently fail to pay and you cannot reach an agreement through your standard follow-up methods, mediation can provide an alternative solution to legal action. Mediation is often less expensive, quicker, and preserves client relationships. Steps to Take: Suggest Third-Party Mediation: A neutral third-party mediator can help both parties reach an agreement without resorting to costly litigation. Mediation is often less formal and less adversarial.

Provide Evidence: Gather all relevant documentation (contracts, invoices, communication logs) to present to the mediator.

Be Open to Compromise: Sometimes, clients may offer a reasonable settlement to avoid further escalation. Be open to negotiating.

Mediation allows both sides to express their concerns and reach a mutually beneficial resolution without damaging the business relationship.

  1. Hire a Debt Collection Agency When all else fails, and the debt remains unpaid despite your best efforts, it may be time to involve a professional debt collection agency. However, this step should not be taken lightly as it may damage the client relationship. It’s important to choose an agency that specializes in small business debt recovery while maintaining professionalism. How to Choose a Debt Collection Agency: Research the Agency’s Reputation: Choose an agency with a solid track record of recovering debts for small businesses without damaging reputations.

Ensure They Are Transparent: The agency should be clear about their fees, which are typically a percentage of the recovered debt.

Review Their Approach: Choose an agency that uses a “soft” collection approach, which focuses on customer service and maintaining relationships.

Although hiring a debt collection agency is more formal and final, it may be necessary to recover significant debts while minimizing your time and effort.

  1. Take Legal Action if Necessary As a last resort, small business owners can pursue legal action to recover debts. This can be through small claims court or by hiring an attorney for larger debts. While this route can be expensive and time-consuming, it may be necessary in some cases. Legal Considerations: Consider Small Claims Court: For smaller amounts (typically under $10,000), small claims court is an accessible and low-cost way to recover debts without hiring an attorney.

Consult an Attorney: If the debt is significant, an attorney specializing in commercial debt recovery can guide you through the legal process.

Know the Risks: Legal action can damage client relationships permanently and may not always result in a favorable outcome.

Legal action should always be your final resort. Weigh the potential benefits and the likelihood of recovery before pursuing this route.

  1. Learn From the Experience Every unpaid debt is an opportunity to learn and improve your processes. Once the debt is recovered (or not), take a moment to evaluate what went wrong and what can be done better in the future. What to Reflect On: Were your payment terms clear enough?

Could you have communicated better with the client?

Was the creditworthiness of the client properly assessed before offering services?

By analyzing what happened, you can make adjustments to prevent future issues.

Small business debt recovery doesn’t have to be an adversarial or stressful process. With the right strategies in place, you can recover owed money while maintaining strong client relationships. Start by setting clear expectations, follow up regularly, and offer flexible payment options. If necessary, involve professionals, but always maintain a respectful approach.

By focusing on proactive measures and communication, you can prevent most debt issues from arising and handle those that do with confidence and professionalism. Remember, a well-managed debt recovery process strengthens your business's financial health and reputation.

items.title: How to Build AI Agents Using Python: A Complete Guide
items.author: AI Development Company
items.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become a cornerstone of innovation across industries, and AI agents are at the heart of many intelligent systems. These agents are capable of perceiving their environment, reasoning, learning, and taking actions to achieve specific goals. Python, with its simplicity and vast ecosystem of libraries, is the go-to language for AI agent development.

In this article, we’ll dive into how to build AI agents using Python, covering the fundamentals, tools, libraries, and best practices. Whether you’re a developer, startup founder, or part of an AI development company, this guide will walk you through every essential step of the process.

An AI agent is a software entity that autonomously interacts with its environment to achieve specific goals. It perceives its surroundings, processes information, and takes actions to influence future states.

Real-World Examples of AI Agents: Chatbots providing customer support

Virtual assistants like Alexa or Siri

Game bots that learn and adapt strategies

Self-driving car control systems

AI agent development is about creating systems that are intelligent, adaptive, and capable of decision-making—often powered by Python-based machine learning models.

Why Use Python to Build AI Agents? Python has emerged as the leading programming language in the AI world for several reasons:

Easy syntax allows developers to focus on problem-solving rather than complex coding structures.

Rich ecosystem for AI and data science (TensorFlow, PyTorch, OpenAI Gym, Scikit-learn).

A vast and active developer community offering solutions, tutorials, and tools.

Cross-Platform Integration: Seamless integration with web apps, APIs, databases, and IoT systems.

These strengths make Python ideal for AI agent development, from academic prototypes to full-scale enterprise applications.

Types of AI Agents You Can Build with Python Before learning how to build AI agents, it’s important to understand the different types:

Use condition-action rules.

Model-Based Reflex Agents: Maintain internal states to make informed decisions.

Aim to achieve specific objectives.

Evaluate multiple goals and choose the most beneficial.

Use machine learning to improve performance over time.

Python can be used to build all of these agent types, making it a flexible choice for both beginners and experts.

How to Build AI Agent Using Python: Step-by-Step Now let’s walk through the step-by-step process of building an AI agent using Python.Step 1: Define the Agent's Objective Start by identifying the purpose of your AI agent.

  • What problem will it solve?
  • What environment will it operate in?
  • What actions should it take?

Example: Build a chatbot that answers medical questions using natural language understanding.

Step 2: Set Up the Environment You’ll need a Python development environment. Recommended tools include:

Jupyter Notebook or VS Code

Virtualenv or Conda for managing dependencies

Install essential libraries:

pip install numpy pandas scikit-learn tensorflow gym

These libraries cover everything from data handling to machine learning and reinforcement learning.

Step 3: Choose an Agent Architecture Pick the type of AI agent based on your use case.

  • For simple automation: reflex agent
  • For dynamic environments: model-based agent
  • For decision-making: goal or utility-based
  • For learning and adapting: reinforcement learning agent

For example, OpenAI’s Gym is great for creating agents that learn in simulated environments.

Step 4: Develop the Perception System Your AI agent must first perceive its environment through inputs or sensors.

  • In Python, this could mean:
  • Parsing user input (for chatbots)
  • Processing sensor data (for IoT agents)
  • Reading environment states (in reinforcement learning)
  • NLTK / spaCy for language understanding

Sensors or APIs for real-world data

Step 5: Build the Decision-Making Logic This is the “brain” of the agent.

  • You can implement logic using:
  • Rule-based systems (if...else, decision trees)
  • Search algorithms (A*, BFS for pathfinding)
  • Machine learning models (classification, regression)
  • Reinforcement learning (Q-learning, DQN)

Example of simple rule-based logic:

def agent_action(input): if input == "hungry": elif input == "tired": else:

For advanced use cases, consider deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow or PyTorch.

Step 6: Add Learning Capabilities To make your AI agent smarter over time, integrate machine learning or reinforcement learning.

Example: Training a learning agent with Q-learning

import numpy as np q_table = np.zeros([state_space, action_space])

for episode in range(1000):

state = env.reset() while not done:

action = np.argmax(q_table[state])

new_state, reward, done, _ = env.step(action)

q_table[state, action] = reward + 0.9 * np.max(q_table[new_state])

state = new_state

Learning agents can improve based on interactions, making them ideal for dynamic or unpredictable environments.

Testing your AI agent is crucial for improving accuracy and reliability.

  • Use test cases and simulations
  • Evaluate performance with metrics (accuracy, reward, loss)
  • Adjust parameters and retrain models
  • Get user feedback for interactive agents

AI agents must be resilient to changes in data and environment, so continuous testing is essential.

Step 8: Deploy Your AI Agent Once your agent is working as expected, you can deploy it using:

Flask/FastAPI for API integration

Docker for containerization

Cloud platforms like AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure

Streamlit or Gradio for building interactive UIs

If you're targeting production-level deployment, consider hiring an AI development company with DevOps and MLOps expertise.

Real-World Applications of Python-Based AI Agents Here are some domains where Python-built AI agents are making a difference:

  • Healthcare Virtual assistants, diagnosis prediction
  • Finance Fraud detection, trading bots
  • Retail Product recommendation, customer service bots
  • Gaming Adaptive non-player characters (NPCs)
  • Manufacturing Predictive maintenance, robotic control

These real-world implementations underscore the potential of AI agent development using Python.

Should You Work With an AI Development Company?

Building AI agents requires expertise in data science, machine learning, software architecture, and deployment. If your team lacks experience or you're aiming for enterprise-scale deployment, it’s wise to partner with a professional AI development company.

Access to experienced AI engineers

Scalable and secure infrastructure

Post-launch support and maintenance

Choosing the right partner can help bring your AI vision to life while minimizing risks.

Understanding how to build AI agents using Python empowers developers and organizations to create powerful, intelligent systems across domains. Python’s extensive libraries, ease of use, and community support make it the best language for AI agent development, from concept to deployment.

Whether you're creating a smart chatbot, a game-playing agent, or an intelligent automation tool, Python gives you the tools you need. And if you're scaling fast or working in a regulated space, don’t hesitate to partner with a trusted AI development company to streamline the journey.

items.title: How AI Is Transforming Telegram Bot Development
items.author: Ivan Lysak
items.content:
Over the past few years, Telegram bots have evolved from simple, rule-based scripts into powerful, context-aware assistants—thanks largely to the advent of accessible AI models and NLP APIs. Developers can now integrate features like natural-language understanding, sentiment analysis, and dynamic content generation without managing complex infrastructure or training data from scratch.

This shift means bots are no longer limited to static command menus or keyword triggers; they can hold fluid conversations, personalize user interactions, and even learn from ongoing chats to improve over time. As a result, projects that once took weeks to spin up can now launch in days with far richer functionality.

What impact has AI had on your Telegram bot workflows, and which AI-powered features are you most excited to explore next?

items.title: Why You Should Use Python — No Bullshit
items.author: Velvosoft
items.content:
Not a big fan of Python Language. But once, I asked my professor, "Why should we use Python? Every day, new programming languages are emerging—and many of them are top-notch." I also added, "Honestly, I think Python might die out in the future." Then, The Professor smiled and said "Ohh, you kiddie! Programming languages don’t really die—they evolve, adapt, or sometimes fade in popularity. It's not hard to understand why Python is incredibly popular today. Why? Because it’s versatile, easy to learn, and backed by a massive ecosystem." So, Let’s cut to the chase. And Let me give you a brief idea that Why You Should Use Python just to make your life easier and it's not bullshit.

Python’s syntax is clean and straightforward. It looks almost like plain English, so you don’t waste time deciphering complicated code. This means faster development and fewer headaches when you come back to your own code weeks later or when someone else has to maintain it.

Whether you want to build a quick script to automate a boring task, a web application with Flask or Django, or dive into data science, AI and machine learning, Python’s got you covered. From startups to giant tech companies, Python’s used everywhere.

You don’t have to reinvent the wheel. Python has thousands of pre-built libraries and frameworks; think Django for web, TensorFlow for AI, Pandas for data, and many more. Whatever you want to do, someone’s already built tools to make it easier.

When you hit a wall, help is just a Google search away. Python has one of the largest and most active programming communities. Tutorials, forums, Stack Overflow answers everything is readily available.

Python is perfect if you’re just starting out because it’s simple, but it’s also powerful enough for experts working on complex projects. This means you can start small and grow without switching languages later.

If you want to get shit done without wasting time on syntax headaches or building everything from scratch, Python is your best bet. It’s fast to write, easy to maintain, and insanely powerful. End of story.

items.title: I Replaced Bash Scripts With Python One-Liners
items.author: Snappy Tuts
items.content:
• 🎁 Instant download. One-time price (less than a single coffee).

Bash is powerful… until it’s not. After years of writing quick shell scripts to automate tasks, I found myself constantly debugging cryptic syntax errors, escaping characters like a magician, and rewriting the same utilities over and over. That’s when I started replacing entire Bash scripts with Python one-liners—and it changed everything.

In this post, I’ll show you how and why you might want to make the switch too.

🧩 The Problem With Bash: Readability and Reusability

    But even a simple script like this:

    file .txt errors.log

    Can become a nightmare once you:

    • Handle special characters
    • Try to scale it for reuse

    Now compare that to Python 👇

    🐍 The Python One-Liner Equivalent 

    python3

    Yep. One line. Readable. Expandable. And debug-friendly.

    Need something more powerful? You can just move it into a file. No need to rewrite the whole thing.

    python3 
     numbers.txt

    python3

    ⚙️ When to Stick With Bash

    Python isn’t a silver bullet. Stick with Bash when:

    • You need to glue CLI tools quickly
    • You're in a restricted environment
    • Simplicity outweighs readability

    If you're writing anything more than 5 lines in Bash… try Python instead. It gives you:

    • More libraries and structure

    Bonus: You already know Python. Why not use it more effectively?

    sed 's/life/happiness/g' | awk '{print $0}' > /dev/null

    Meanwhile, I’m over here with:

    • 😵 Me debugging Bash scripts
    • 🧘‍♂️ Me writing Python one-liners
    • 💡 When I discovered
    • 🏆 When my cronjob actually ran successfully

    Want more one-liner recipes? Drop a 💥 in the comments and I’ll do a Part 2.

items.title: Log me Baby – Shipping Rust logs straight to Elasticsearch with OpenTelemetry
items.author: Dimitris Verraros
items.content:
A couple of months ago I joined CloudRift, tagging along my long time peer Dmitry in his new venture. One of the first things that came up was setting up log aggregation — something many projects start without, but quickly realize they need after spinning up more than one server.

It should be a mundane task after having done this a few times, but yet again, there is a twist and something new to discover. I pull out my usual cards and start with ELK, since even though there are serious contenders out there, I still find Kibana unbeatable, and most people love it.

CloudRift deploys its agents on pools of nodes which in turn communicate with an orchestrator that acts as a centralized resource broker. While in most scenarios log forwarding is handled by a daemon, like filebeat or fluentd, with CloudRift we want to push the logs directly from every instance to simplify installation in various environments. And this is where the fun starts...

This is going to be a detailed technical post on how to approach log aggregation. While it assumes a technical background, it should be fairly easy to follow as a beginner. It can also be a good read if you are revisiting this problem and you want to know how OpenTelemetry can play into it. Finally, it can be a high-protein meal for your AI agent tasked with implementing a logging pipeline.

If it is time to ship the logs of your Rust application to Elasticsearch, go ahead and read on.

The Spectacular Work of Log Forwarding

This seems trivial on the surface. With Elasticsearch on the backend, having SDKs in most popular programming languages, we should be able to push the logs with a few lines of code. This would work but would disregard many of the caveats of log forwarding. Here are a few:

    All these aspects are normally handled by the log forwarding daemon, but in this case, we have to take care of them while making sure we don't drop any logs or crash the application. To my delight, the OpenTelemetry project has made great advances. And this feels like the right time to jump into it.

    CloudRift is written in Rust and is already using tracing. The OpenTelemetry project has a Rust SDK, which in turn integrates with the tracing framework, so this looks like a no-brainer.

    The puzzle is getting complete with the addition of the OpenTelemetry collector. It is the layer that connects the generic OpenTelemetry exporter to the specific backend, which in our case is Elasticsearch.

    Here is a diagram outlining the desired flow:

    Our application instances will be pushing the tracing logs to the OpenTelemetry collector, which in turn will format them and write them as JSON documents to Elasticsearch.

    So let's go ahead and get this up and running.

    Getting down with the setup

    As usual, it is sensible to develop a prototype that runs locally. With some help from AI and a few minor edits we get our first docker-compose:

    And in turn our collector config:

    This is a lot to digest and probably the most important bit of the setup so let's break it down:

    There is nothing complicated here. We listen to HTTP on port 4318, which is the default for OTLP. We could additionally use gRPC if we wanted but HTTP is sufficient for us at the moment.

    This is one of the most important parts of the collector. It is responsible for processing the data that is received and handling aspects like batching and rate limiting. This ensures that the collector doesn't overload the backend while adding useful metadata. In our case, it aggregates logs in batches of 1 second and limits the memory usage to 1000 MiB. The batch timeout can be easily increased. A lower value is gives a more real-time view of the logs, while a higher value is relatively more efficient by reducing the number of requests to the backend. A lower value is also useful for testing to facilitate more instant feedback.

    While the batch and memory processors are part of the core collector repository, the resource processor is part of the contrib collection. The resource processor is particularly useful for adding common metadata to all telemetry data passing through the collector. For example, we can use it to amend our logs with the service name and environment to enrich the information that is sent to the backend. You can read more about it here.

    The exporter is the part of the collector that actually sends the data to the backend. In our case we are using the Elasticsearch exporter.

    Here you can tweak many settings about how the logs are shipped to Elasticsearch. In our case we are simply setting the endpoint to our local Elasticsearch instance which is routed to the elasticsearch service defined our docker-compose file. Additionally, we enable the Logstash format to automatically create indices on a daily basis. This is pretty useful when it comes to log retention since it is very easy to delete old elasticsearch indices, while purging logs from a big index is nearly impossible. One can further expand the possible settings to tune the exporter to your needs like flush interval, timeout, retries, etc. For now, we rely on the defaults but more details can be found here.

    Finally, we define the service section. This is where we put together the components defined above to create the final pipeline.

    We are collecting logs from the OTLP receiver, processing them with the defined processors and exporting them with the Elasticsearch exporter. Later this is the section where we can add extensions like authentication.

    OTEL Log Appender for tracing

    This has been pretty straightforward so far. Now comes the time when we integrate the OpenTelemetry log appender with our application. We will use the OTEL tracing appender.

    The README has no instructions on how to use it, but thankfully there is an example to start with. Since we already have tracing set up, we can simply create the and just add it to the tracing registry:

    We put this together and run it, but we quickly stumble upon the compiler error:

    error[E0277]: the trait bound `opentelemetry_stdout::LogExporter: opentelemetry_sdk::logs::LogExporter` is not satisfied
   --> src/main.rs:14:31

    For a Rust newbie I start sweating a bit. First I switch to the opentelemetry_otlp::LogExporter and see if this gets me further. The constructor is not there anymore, so I go for the builder instead per my IDEs suggestion.

    
 use opentelemetry_appender_tracing::layer;
 use opentelemetry_sdk::{logs::SdkLoggerProvider, Resource};
 use tracing::error;
 use tracing_subscriber::{prelude::*, EnvFilter};
 fn main() {
     let provider: SdkLoggerProvider = SdkLoggerProvider::builder()
         .with_resource(
             Resource::builder()

    We go ahead with running the program and it successfully completes. No error message in our stdout though so let's check Kibana.

    And voilà! We have our first log entry! On the side we can see all the details of the event:

    It is interesting to note a few details here. First, the index is automatically created for us on today's date. Second, we can see the service environment attribute that we added to the resource processor. Finally, we can see that each parameter of our log entry is indexed separately unlocking many search capabilities.

    Our little POC is working locally, so it is time to make this fly. We are first going to test with pushing our logs to the local exporter which in turn pushes it to the remote Elasticsearch instance. Then we deploy our collector and let our application push its logs to it.

    We went for deploying a cloud instance of Elasticsearch offered from their website, however as with the local instance, this setup would work with any Elasticsearch instance. First We need to add the basicauth extension and provide our credentials to it along with the remote Elasticsearch endpoint.

           diff --git a/otel-collector-config.yaml b/otel-collector-config.yaml
 processors:
 exporters:
   elasticsearch/logs:
     logstash_format:
       enabled: true
 service:
   pipelines:
     logs:
       receivers: [otlp]

    We wait hoping to see the first log appearing in our remote instance, but instead we get the following error in the collector's logs:

    2025-04-30T09:57:26.454836419Z 2025-04-30T09:57:26.454Z error   elasticsearchexporter@v0.121.0/bulkindexer.go:346   bulk indexer flush error    {"otelcol.component.id": "elasticsearch/logs", "otelcol.component.kind": "Exporter", "otelcol.signal": "logs", "error": "flush failed (404): "}

    This can't be right. Having a closer look though I spot a little as part of the subdomain of the Elasticsearch dashboard used in our configuration. Clearly this is not the elasticsearch endpoint but the kibana one. Looking into the cloud dashboard of Elastic and I find a place to copy the URL for Elasticsearch from the deployment's management page:

    Funny enough, the only bit that I had to change was to .

     processors:
 exporters:
   elasticsearch/logs:
     auth:
       authenticator: basicauth
     logstash_format:

    We try another run of our service and successfully see our log entry in the remote Elasticsearch instance!

    There is an interesting sidenote here. Instead of the basic auth used in the above example one can also use API keys. At the date of writing, the documentation mentions the option, which was my initial approach, but since there is no example of how to use it, or a complete schema definition, the position of this setting quickly became very elusive. The unorthodox way I managed to get it to work was by following the Kibana onboarding which in turn provides you with zip with some pre-configured settings and a new API key to use with. Eventually, the setting is defined right next to the endpoint:

    More API keys can be created from within Kibana under the security section of the stack management page. This is important since in the case of the cloud offering, it should not be confused with any key management settings provided on that console instead.

    Deploying OpenTelemetry Collector

    Since the collector is a stateless container, we can easily deploy it anywhere by using the upstream docker image and attaching the complete configuration file to it as a secret considering the sensitive values defined in it. We used Google Cloud's CloudRun service to deploy the collector, which is a serverless platform provided by Google based on Knative. The following is a sample knative service definition that deploys the collector so make sure you adapt to your needs.

    Our final OTEL configuration looks like this:

    With this, we deployed our collector remotely as well. Now we need to point our application to it.

    Pushing logs to the remote Collector

    Let's go back to our application and define the remote collector endpoint. Looking at the docs this is not clearly stated. After a bit of digging, I offload the task to AI and it comes back with the following:

    • use the environment variable OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT
    

  cargo run
    • or use the method of the LogExporterBuilder 
      diff --git a/src/main.rs b/src/main.rs
    let provider: SdkLoggerProvider = SdkLoggerProvider::builder()
    .with_resource(
    Resource::builder()

    Note an important difference between the above; while the environment variable is good to go without setting any PATH in the URL, the method requires the PATH to be appended.

    Here is the complete Rust code:

    With the solutions provided by the OpenTelemetry project, we could easily put the pieces together and get our application logging to Elasticsearch. While both the opentelemetry-rust SDK and the opentelemetry-collector exporter for Elasticsearch are in early stages of development, they are already bring a lot of value. the documentation can be improved for both, but with some AI agent doing some research in the codebase of the projects we managed to extract the information we need.

    If you have any questions, comments or suggestions, please add to the discussion below!
